E-Learning Challenge #557
Choose Your Destination
Six floating islands. Six distinct design philosophies.
Click any island on the map to dive into that world and explore what makes each style unique.
Once inside a world, use the ← → arrows to travel seamlessly between worlds, or hit the Map button to return here at any time.

World 1 of 6
Neumorphism
AI Image Prompt
REPLACE_WITH_ACTUAL_PROMPT_TEXT
Neumorphism builds every element out of the same base color, using only light and shadow to fake depth — like the UI is carved from a single slab of material. I like it because it feels quiet and tactile in a way flat design never does; when I want an interface to feel soft and premium without leaning on color, this is where I go. I used it recently on a Storyline preference-center prototype for the inputs and persona cards before evolving the look further.
Limitations
Notoriously bad for accessibility — the whole effect depends on low contrast between elements and their background, which fails WCAG contrast ratios almost by design. Great for a polished demo, risky for anything that actually needs to ship to real learners.
World 2 of 6
Glassmorphism
AI Image Prompt
REPLACE_WITH_ACTUAL_PROMPT_TEXT
Glassmorphism layers translucent, blurred panels over a colorful background so content looks like frosted glass floating above the scene. I like it for screens that need to feel modern and a little high-end — dashboards, onboarding flows, anything where you want depth without going full 3D. I leaned on it briefly on the same Storyline build, frosting the input boxes before landing on a more solid-fill look.
Limitations
Text over blur can get hard to read fast, especially for low-vision users, and backdrop blur is genuinely expensive to render — it can lag on older devices or in an iframe-heavy environment like a published Storyline course.
Enter the bell tower →
World 3 of 6
Skeuomorphism
AI Image Prompt
REPLACE_WITH_ACTUAL_PROMPT_TEXT
Skeuomorphism makes digital elements mimic real-world objects — leather textures, stitching, glossy buttons that look physically pressable. I like it for moments where the metaphor genuinely helps someone understand what to do, like a toggle switch that actually looks like a switch. I don't reach for full skeuomorphism often, but I'll borrow small skeuomorphic cues — a drop shadow that implies "this is a button" — even inside a flatter overall design.
Limitations
Full skeuomorphism ages badly and gets visually heavy fast — every added texture is more weight on the page, and it can read as dated or kitschy within a couple years rather than aging like a classic.
Skeuomorphism — Brassford

Ring the Bell

World 4 of 6
Futurism
AI Image Prompt
REPLACE_WITH_ACTUAL_PROMPT_TEXT
Futurism leans on glowing accents, sharp geometry, and a sense of motion — sleek HUD-style panels, grid overlays, that "you're inside a control system" feeling. I like it for anything that wants to feel cutting-edge or technical — dashboards monitoring something live, or onboarding for a more advanced tool where "serious and capable" matters more than "warm and friendly."
Limitations
The glow and grid effects lean heavily on layered shadows and blurs, which can get expensive to render at scale, and the aesthetic can tip into "trying too hard" if every single element is glowing — it works best when restraint picks a couple of focal points rather than lighting up the whole screen.
World 5 of 6
Vaporwave
AI Image Prompt
REPLACE_WITH_ACTUAL_PROMPT_TEXT
Vaporwave pulls from 80s/90s retro-futurism — pink and purple gradients, a glowing sunset, perspective grid lines, a general "nostalgic dream" mood. I like it because it's pure personality with zero pretense of being a serious productivity tool; it's a style that says "have fun" before you've even read anything. I'd reach for it on something playful — a fun internal contest page, a culture-and-recognition tool, anything that benefits from not feeling corporate.
Limitations
It's almost entirely mood with very little built-in legibility — busy gradients and glow effects behind text fight readability fast, so it needs careful contrast checking, and it's wildly mismatched for anything that needs to feel credible or serious.
World 6 of 6
Brutalism
AI Image Prompt
REPLACE_WITH_ACTUAL_PROMPT_TEXT
Brutalism strips everything down to raw structure — harsh contrast, visible grid lines, thick borders, no soft edges or apologies for how blunt it looks. I like it because it's the opposite of every "trust us, we're friendly" SaaS interface out there; it reads as confident and unbothered rather than trying to win you over. I'd reach for it on something that wants to feel intentionally different — internal tools, dev-facing dashboards, anything where "approachable" isn't actually the goal.
Limitations
It can come across as actively hostile if the audience isn't in on the bit — what feels like a deliberate stylistic choice to a design-literate viewer can just look broken or unfinished to a general learner, so it's a risky default for anything aimed at a wide or non-technical audience.
Also
A real brutalist layout wouldn't leave this much breathing room — it'd just drop another box right on top, clashing on purpose.
